era_context: The Classical Greek world during the early decades following the Persian Wars (480–479 BCE). Athens is consolidating its naval power and beginning its rise to hegemony in the Aegean under the leadership of figures like Cimon and later Pericles. The Delian League, ostensibly a defensive alliance against Persian aggression, is transforming into an Athenian empire. Simultaneously, Sparta maintains land dominance in the Peloponnese, and tensions between these two powers are mounting. In the East, the Achaemenid Persian Empire under Artaxerxes I is stabilizing after internal succession struggles.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond Greek and Roman contact zones
Central Asia and the Steppe (limited sources for this period)
East Asia beyond fragmentary Chinese records

events.title: Revolt of the Egyptian satrap Inarus against Persian rule
events.description: Inarus, a Libyan prince, led a major rebellion against Achaemenid Persian control of Egypt, with support from Athens. The revolt was part of a broader pattern of resistance to Persian imperial authority in the eastern Mediterranean. Athenian naval forces under Charitimides were dispatched to support the rebellion, marking an early instance of Athenian intervention in Persian-controlled territories. The rebellion was ultimately suppressed by Persian forces under Megabyzus around 454 BCE, but the initial uprising in 465 BCE represents a significant moment of anti-Persian mobilization.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book I

events.certainty_note: The rebellion is well-attested in Greek sources, but the precise dating of its outbreak is uncertain. Thucydides places it in the context of the 460s BCE, but exact chronology depends on reconstruction of the Athenian archon list and Egyptian chronology.

events.title: Ostracism of Cimon at Athens
events.description: Cimon, the prominent Athenian general and statesman who had led successful campaigns against the Persians and expanded Athenian naval power, was ostracized (exiled for ten years) by popular vote. This removal from power marked a significant shift in Athenian politics, as it reflected growing opposition to his pro-Spartan foreign policy and his dominance in Athenian affairs. The ostracism paved the way for the rise of more radical democratic figures and those favoring an anti-Spartan stance. The precise date of Cimon's ostracism is debated among scholars, with some placing it in 461 BCE, but 465 BCE is also proposed by some ancient sources.

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War

events.certainty_note: The date of Cimon's ostracism is one of the most disputed chronological questions in Classical Athenian history. Ancient sources give conflicting dates (461 vs. 465 BCE). Modern scholarship is divided, with some favoring 461 BCE based on the archon list reconstruction.
cross_references: Led to rise of Pericles in subsequent years

events.title: Earthquake and helot revolt in Sparta
events.description: A major earthquake struck the Spartan region, causing significant destruction and loss of life. The disaster triggered or exacerbated a revolt among the helots (enslaved population) of Sparta, who saw the catastrophe as an opportunity to rebel against their masters. The helot uprising, combined with the earthquake's devastation, severely weakened Spartan military capacity and forced the state to seek external assistance. This event had major geopolitical consequences, as it temporarily reduced Spartan power and altered the balance of forces in Greece.
key_figures: Archidamus II (Spartan king during the crisis)

sources.name: Thucydides, History of the Peloponnesian War, Book I

events.certainty_note: The earthquake is well-attested in ancient sources, and geological evidence supports major seismic activity in the Spartan region during the mid-5th century BCE. However, the precise year is uncertain; sources variously place it between 465–464 BCE.
cross_references: Led to -464-001 (helot revolt continuation and Athenian intervention)

disconfirming_evidence: The dating of Cimon's ostracism remains highly contested. While traditionally placed in 465 BCE by some scholars, the majority of modern scholarship now favors 461 BCE based on more rigorous reconstruction of the Athenian archon list. The earthquake and helot revolt are well-attested, but the precise year (465 vs. 464 BCE) is debated. Some sources suggest the earthquake occurred in 464 BCE, making the dating of the initial seismic event uncertain by approximately one year.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 465 BCE is dominated by Athenian and Greek sources, particularly Thucydides and Plutarch, both writing decades or centuries after the events. There is a significant Western bias in surviving documentation. Egyptian and Persian perspectives on the Inarus rebellion are largely lost, known only through Greek accounts. The dating of events in this period depends heavily on the reconstruction of the Athenian archon list, which itself contains uncertainties. Archaeological evidence for the earthquake is more reliable than textual dating.

graph_edges.note: The Inarus rebellion was eventually suppressed by Persian forces under Megabyzus
The earthquake triggered or exacerbated the helot revolt that continued into 464 BCE
Cimon's ostracism may have occurred in 461 BCE rather than 465 BCE; the events are related to the same political realignment
